questions.question: Select all appropriate initial management steps for a patient complaining of posterior neck pain after a motor vehicle accident.
choices: Consider imaging if there is midline posterior cervical tenderness or inability to actively rotate the neck 45 degrees to either side
Consider imaging if neurological symptoms are present in the extremities
Assess whether the patient can reliably report cervical symptoms (e.g., distracting pain elsewhere, intoxication, altered mental status, dementia)
Rear-end collision into a stationary vehicle is always considered a high-risk mechanism
If imaging is normal, prescribe 1 month of cervical collar immobilization for ligamentous injury and disc rest
questions.explanation: Cervical sprain is diagnosed only after excluding significant cervical injury. Use tools such as the Canadian C-Spine Rule to assess age, mechanism, and extremity numbness, and obtain imaging as needed. Analgesia, guidance to avoid excessive rest, and instructions to return if worsening are appropriate when no bony injury or neurological deficit is found. Imaging cannot be omitted solely based on neck pain, and prolonged cervical collar immobilization is generally not recommended.

questions.question: Select the 3 appropriate discharge instructions for a patient with cervical sprain.
choices: Pain may persist for a few days
Continue daily activities as tolerated
Return for evaluation if numbness or muscle weakness develops
In the absence of fracture, begin aggressive massage promptly after injury
Immobilize the neck with a collar until pain resolves and maintain complete rest
questions.explanation: After cervical sprain, pain may persist or temporarily worsen for several days. Instruct the patient to maintain daily activities as tolerated while using analgesics. Return promptly if limb numbness, weakness, gait disturbance, or urinary symptoms develop, as these may indicate spinal cord or nerve root injury. Even without fracture on imaging, follow-up instructions are necessary; complete rest may delay recovery.

questions.question: Select all findings that warrant consideration of plain radiographs for ankle sprain.
choices: Bony tenderness over the posterior 6 cm or tip of the lateral malleolus
Bony tenderness over the posterior 6 cm or tip of the medial malleolus
Inability to bear weight for 4 steps immediately after injury or in the ED
Bony tenderness at the base of the fifth metatarsal or navicular bone of the foot
Tenderness only over the anterior talofibular ligament
questions.explanation: The Ottawa Ankle Rules guide imaging decisions. Ankle X-ray is indicated if there is ankle pain with bony tenderness over the posterior 6 cm or tip of the lateral or medial malleolus, or inability to bear weight for 4 steps immediately after injury or in the ED. Foot X-ray is indicated if midfoot pain is present with bony tenderness at the base of the fifth metatarsal or navicular, or inability to bear weight for 4 steps. Isolated anterior talofibular ligament tenderness alone is generally not an indication. However, individual clinical judgment is needed for elderly patients, children, those with neuropathy, altered mental status, intoxication, unreliable exam, significant swelling/deformity, or high clinical suspicion of fracture.

questions.question: Select the 3 appropriate initial management steps for ankle sprain.
choices: Apply Ottawa Ankle Rules: check for bony tenderness over the posterior 6 cm or tip of the lateral/medial malleolus, inability to bear weight for 4 steps, and bony tenderness at the base of the fifth metatarsal or navicular
Perform CT on all patients with tenderness or swelling over the anterior talofibular ligament even if ambulation is possible
Even for ankle sprain, check for proximal fibula pain and lower leg tenderness, and consider imaging including the knee and lower leg if positive
If fracture is unlikely, provide analgesia, ice, compression, elevation, and short-term external fixation or bracing as needed, promoting early functional recovery as tolerated
In the acute phase with significant swelling, apply a tight circumferential cast and maintain complete rest for 1 month to prevent re-swelling
questions.explanation: Initial management of ankle sprain begins by excluding fracture and other injuries not to miss. Ottawa Ankle Rules guide imaging decisions. Proximal fibula pain, interosseous membrane tenderness, or pain with external rotation stress should raise concern for Maisonneuve fracture or high ankle syndesmosis injury, prompting imaging that includes the lower leg. If fracture is unlikely, treat with analgesia, icing, compression, elevation, and short-term immobilization or bracing, favoring early functional rehabilitation over prolonged complete rest. Tight circumferential casting in the acute swollen phase risks pain, numbness, circulatory compromise, and compartment syndrome.

questions.question: Select all correct statements about splinting and functional positioning.
choices: The wrist is splinted in slight dorsiflexion
The elbow is often splinted at approximately 90 degrees of flexion
The ankle is splinted at approximately 90 degrees (neutral position)
The MCP joints should always be splinted in extension
The knee must always be splinted in deep flexion of 90 degrees or more, never in full extension
questions.explanation: Splinting aims to immobilize in a position that minimizes functional impairment and contracture after assessing injury, swelling, pain, and neurovascular status. For hand injuries requiring finger immobilization, the intrinsic-plus position (wrist slight dorsiflexion, MCP flexion, IP extension) is the standard. The elbow is commonly splinted at ~90 degrees of flexion but the angle may be adjusted if vascular compromise or significant swelling is present. The ankle is splinted in neutral (approximately 90 degrees). Splinting the MCP joints in extension can cause contracture and is not the standard. The knee may be splinted in slight flexion or near extension depending on the pathology, and deep flexion is not always required. After splinting, reassess pain, numbness, skin color, temperature, capillary refill, and motor function.

questions.question: Select the ONE CORRECT statement regarding fall-related trauma in the elderly.
choices: In patients aged ≥65 with head trauma, minor head injury with loss of consciousness, amnesia, or disorientation is a high-risk indication for head CT, and this age group is also high-risk for cervical spine injury requiring imaging
Thoracolumbar compression fractures are most common at L3–L5
For suspected distal radius fracture, a two-view forearm X-ray alone is sufficient
Among proximal femur fractures, intertrochanteric fractures often result in avascular necrosis of the femoral head
Most shoulder pain in elderly patients is due to shoulder dislocation
questions.explanation: The Canadian CT Head Rule identifies age ≥65 as a high-risk factor for head CT in minor head injury with loss of consciousness, amnesia, or disorientation. The Canadian C-Spine Rule also identifies age ≥65 as a high-risk factor requiring cervical imaging. Vertebral compression fractures are most common at the thoracolumbar junction, particularly around T12–L1. Distal radius fracture requires wrist AP and lateral views as a baseline. Avascular necrosis is mainly a concern with femoral neck fractures, not typically intertrochanteric fractures. Elderly shoulder trauma frequently involves proximal humerus fractures, so shoulder dislocation alone is not the predominant diagnosis.

questions.question: Which ONE of the following is NOT part of the '5 Ps' indicating possible vascular injury in extremity trauma?
choices: Pain
Pallor
Pulselessness
Paresthesia
Pitting edema
questions.explanation: The 5 Ps are Pain, Pallor, Pulselessness, Paresthesia, and Paralysis. These are assessed during the secondary survey when examining the extremities. Pitting edema is not one of the 5 Ps.

questions.question: Which ONE of the following statements about upper extremity trauma is CORRECT?
choices: For shoulder pain, a single AP view of the shoulder joint is sufficient, and evaluation of the clavicle and proximal humerus is unnecessary
The most common location of clavicle fracture is the medial one-third
When performing traction reduction for shoulder dislocation using the zero-position technique, traction is applied at 30 degrees of shoulder abduction
For elbow trauma, no obvious fracture was seen on X-ray but a posterior fat pad sign was positive, so the patient was managed as a fracture
In adults who fall on an outstretched hand, the most common elbow injury is supracondylar fracture of the humerus
questions.explanation: Shoulder pain can originate from the shoulder joint, clavicle, or cervical spine. Clavicle fractures most commonly occur in the middle one-third. Zero-position shoulder reduction involves traction at 130–160 degrees of abduction. In adult elbow trauma, radial head fracture is common and often missed, but a positive posterior fat pad sign suggests an occult fracture, and the patient should be managed accordingly.

questions.question: Select ALL correct statements about nursemaid's elbow (pulled elbow).
choices: It results from the radial head slipping partially out of the annular ligament
It occurs in children aged 5 years or younger
It most often occurs as a result of a fall
Reduction is performed by supinating the forearm while pressing on the radial head, and pronation is contraindicated
After reduction, the child may not move the arm immediately; observe for 10–30 minutes
questions.explanation: Nursemaid's elbow results from subluxation of the radial head through the annular ligament and is seen in children aged ≤5. It typically occurs when the arm is pulled, not from a fall or direct trauma; if a fall or direct blow is reported, consider fracture and obtain X-rays. Reduction can be performed by either the supination-flexion or hyperpronation method; pronation is not contraindicated. After reduction, the child may not move the arm immediately, so observe for approximately 10–30 minutes.
